eli3: Simple Random Sampling (SRS) selects n observations from a population of N so that every possible sample of size n is equally likely to be chosen. SRS without replacement (SRSWOR) — each unit selected at most once — is the standard; SRS with replacement (SRSWR) allows repeated selection and is the basis for bootstrap methods.

The sample mean x̄ is an unbiased estimator of the population mean μ for SRSWOR: E[x̄] = μ. Its variance is Var(x̄) = σ²/n · (1 − n/N) — the factor (1−n/N) is the finite population correction (FPC). For large populations (n/N < 0.05), the FPC ≈ 1 and Var(x̄) ≈ σ²/n, the same as iid sampling. For large sampling fractions, the FPC substantially reduces variance.

In practice, true SRS is hard — it requires a complete sampling frame (list of all population members). Systematic sampling (select every k-th unit after a random start) and cluster sampling (randomly select clusters, then sample within clusters) are practical alternatives that approximate SRS under certain conditions.

whenWhy.title: Foundation for all probability sampling
whenWhy.body: SRS is the theoretical benchmark against which other sampling designs are compared. Stratified and cluster sampling are evaluated by how their variance compares to SRS variance at the same total n. The Central Limit Theorem applies to sample means under SRS, justifying normal-based confidence intervals for large samples.

whenWhy.title: When a complete sampling frame exists
whenWhy.body: SRS is feasible when you can list all population members (e.g., employee records, voter rolls, product database). For online experiments (A/B tests), users can be randomly assigned to conditions — effectively SRS with replacement from the user population. When no complete frame exists, alternatives (cluster sampling, respondent-driven sampling) are necessary.

whenWhy.title: Baseline for design effect calculations
whenWhy.body: The design effect (DEFF) = Var(estimator under complex design)/Var(estimator under SRS) measures efficiency loss from departing from SRS. DEFF > 1 for cluster sampling (within-cluster homogeneity increases variance); DEFF < 1 for stratified sampling (between-strata heterogeneity is removed). Survey weights and design-based inference use SRS as the baseline.

deepDiveIntro: Under simple random sampling without replacement, the sample mean is unbiased for the population mean and its variance equals the population variance divided by sample size multiplied by a finite-population correction factor. The correction factor is one minus the sampling fraction and reduces variance whenever a noticeable proportion of the population is sampled. When sampling with replacement, draws are independent and the correction disappears.

The Horvitz-Thompson estimator provides a unified framework for any probability sampling design: each sampled unit's value is divided by its inclusion probability before averaging. For simple random sampling, all inclusion probabilities equal the sampling fraction, so the estimator reduces to the ordinary sample mean.

deepDive.heading: Variance of Sample Mean Under SRSWOR
deepDive.body: Under simple random sampling without replacement, the sample mean is unbiased for the population mean. Its variance is reduced when a noticeable fraction of the population is sampled, because observing one unit gives partial information about the rest. This finite-population effect is what distinguishes survey sampling from idealized independent sampling.

deepDive.heading: Required Sample Size for Desired Margin of Error
deepDive.body: Sample-size planning in surveys starts by choosing how much error you are willing to tolerate. You first compute the size needed under an effectively infinite population and then adjust it downward when the population itself is not very large. This is why finite populations often require fewer observations than the usual textbook formulas suggest.
deepDive.formula: n₀ = (z_{α/2} · σ / e)²,   n = n₀ / (1 + n₀/N)

deepDive.heading: Systematic Sampling
deepDive.body: Systematic sampling is operationally simpler than full simple random sampling because you only need one random start and then follow a fixed interval through the list. It works well when the frame order is effectively random, but it can perform badly when the list has hidden periodic structure. Good frame ordering is therefore crucial for making systematic sampling behave like a random design.

deepDive.heading: Cluster Sampling and Design Effect
deepDive.body: Cluster sampling saves cost by grouping data collection, but it usually sacrifices statistical efficiency. Units within the same cluster tend to resemble one another, so each extra observation contributes less new information than it would under simple random sampling. The design effect summarizes how much extra sample size is needed to compensate for that loss.

takeaways: SRSWOR: E[x̄] = μ, Var(x̄) = σ²(1−f)/n; FPC = (1−n/N) reduces variance for large sampling fractions. [1]
Required n: n₀ = (z_{α/2}·σ/e)² for desired MOE e, adjusted to n = n₀/(1+n₀/N) with FPC. [1]
Systematic sampling is operationally efficient; beware periodicity matching the selection interval. [1]
Cluster sampling: DEFF = 1+(m−1)·ICC; increases needed n by the design effect factor. [1]
HT estimator: Ȳ_HT = (1/N)Σyᵢ/πᵢ is design-unbiased for any probability sampling design. [1]

edgeCases.q: What happens if the sampling frame is incomplete (coverage error)?
edgeCases.a: Coverage error occurs when some population members have no chance of being selected (under-coverage) or some non-members are included (over-coverage). Under-coverage is more common and causes bias: excluded subpopulations are not represented in estimates. Example: telephone surveys undercover households without phones. Solutions: frame augmentation (add missing units), multiplicity sampling, or post-stratification weighting to known population totals via calibration. [1]

Yes — SRS guarantees unbiasedness in expectation, but a specific sample can be unrepresentative. With probability 1, SRS produces a range of possible samples, some more representative than others. Restriction sampling (balanced sampling) can constrain samples to be more representative on auxiliary variables known for all units, but at the cost of randomization-based inference. Stratified sampling is the standard way to guarantee representation of important subgroups. [1]

sources.label: Lohr — Sampling: Design and Analysis (2nd ed.), Ch. 2
